usecases.title: Bank Recon
usecases.description: Identify unmatched transactions between the bank statement and the books using Power Query Merge (Anti Join) — without writing a single VLOOKUP
usecases.objective: Identify unmatched transactions between the bank statement and the books using Power Query Merge (Anti Join) — without writing a single VLOOKUP.

parts.title: PART A — Load the Bank Statement
steps: 1. In Excel: click the DATA tab on the ribbon
2. Click Get Data → From File → From Text/CSV
3. Browse to this folder → select Bank_Statement_SBI_Jun2025.csv → click Import
4. A preview window opens. Check that columns look correct.
5. Click Transform Data (not Load) — this opens Power Query Editor
6. In Power Query Editor, double-click the query name in the left panel
Rename it to: BankStatement
7. Select the Debit (INR) and Credit (INR) and Balance (INR) columns
(hold Ctrl and click each column header)
8. Right-click any selected column → Change Type → Decimal Number
9. Click the Value Date column header
Home tab → Data Type → Date

parts.title: PART B — Load the Book Entries
steps: 10. In Power Query Editor: Home tab → New Source → Text/CSV
11. Browse to this folder → select Book_Entries_Jun2025.csv → click Import
12. Rename the query to: BookEntries
13. Select the Debit (INR) and Credit (INR) columns
Right-click → Change Type → Decimal Number
14. Select the Date column → Home tab → Data Type → Date
15. We only want Payment and Receipt vouchers for recon (not Journal entries)
Click the dropdown arrow on the Voucher Type column header
Uncheck Journal → click OK

parts.title: PART C — Find Bank transactions NOT in Books (Bank-only items)
steps: 16. In the left panel, click BankStatement to make it active
17. Home tab → Merge Queries → Merge Queries as New
(This creates a separate query and keeps BankStatement unchanged)
18. In the Merge dialog:
• Top table is already BankStatement — click the "Ref / Cheque No" column
to highlight it (it turns green)
• Bottom dropdown: select BookEntries
• Click the "Ref / Cheque No" column in BookEntries table
• Join Kind dropdown: select Left Anti (rows only in left table)
• Click OK
19. A new query called "Merge1" appears in the left panel — rename it to: BankOnly
20. DELETE the BookEntries column:
Right-click the "BookEntries" column header (it shows "Table" in every row)
→ Remove Column
This column was only needed for the join — we don't need its contents.
21. Add an Amount column to standardise with BooksOnly later:
Add Column tab → Custom Column
Name: Amount (INR)
Formula: = if [#"Debit (INR)"] <> null then [#"Debit (INR)"]
else [#"Credit (INR)"]
Click OK → Right-click → Change Type → Decimal Number
22. Add Column tab → Custom Column
Name: Exception Type
Formula: = "In Bank — Not in Books"
Click OK
23. Keep only the columns needed for the final report:
Hold Ctrl and click: Value Date, Description, Ref / Cheque No,
Amount (INR), Exception Type
Right-click → Remove Other Columns
24. Rename Value Date column: double-click header → type Date → Enter

parts.title: PART D — Find Book entries NOT in Bank (Book-only items)
steps: 25. In the left panel, click BookEntries to make it active
26. Home tab → Merge Queries → Merge Queries as New
27. In the Merge dialog:
• Top table is already BookEntries — click "Ref / Cheque No"
• Bottom dropdown: select BankStatement
• Click "Ref / Cheque No" in BankStatement
• Join Kind: Left Anti
• Click OK
28. A new query "Merge2" appears — rename it to: BooksOnly
29. DELETE the BankStatement column:
Right-click the "BankStatement" column header (shows "Table" in every row)
→ Remove Column
30. Add an Amount column:
Add Column tab → Custom Column
Name: Amount (INR)
Formula: = if [#"Debit (INR)"] <> null then [#"Debit (INR)"]
else [#"Cr

usecases.title: Multi File Consolidation
usecases.description: Combine 3 monthly sales CSV files into one clean table automatically
usecases.objective: Combine 3 monthly sales CSV files into one clean table automatically. Adding a new month file later requires zero manual work — just Refresh.

parts.title: PART A — Load the Folder
steps: 1. In Excel: click the DATA tab on the ribbon
2. Click Get Data → From File → From Folder
3. Browse to this folder (UC-02-Multi-File-Consolidation) → click OK
4. A preview shows all 3 CSV files listed
5. Click Combine → Combine & Transform Data
(This works here because all 3 files have identical column structure)
6. A "Combine Files" dialog opens — it shows a preview of the first file
Leave all settings as default → click OK
7. Power Query Editor opens with all 3 files already combined (~300 rows)

parts.title: PART B — Review the Auto-Created Queries
steps: 8. Look at the left panel — you will see multiple queries:
• Transform Sample File — Power Query's template query
• Sales_Apr2025.csv — individual file query (helper)
• Sales_May2025.csv — individual file query (helper)
• Sales_Jun2025.csv — individual file query (helper)
• The main query (your combined table) — at the top
9. Click the main combined query in the left panel
10. You will see a column called Source.Name — this shows which file
each row came from

parts.title: PART C — Clean Up
steps: 11. Right-click the Source.Name column header → Rename
Type: Source File → press Enter
12. Select Invoice Date column
Home tab → Data Type → Date
13. Hold Ctrl and select these columns:
Unit Price (INR), Net Amount (INR), GST Amount (INR), Total Invoice Value (INR)
Right-click → Change Type → Decimal Number
14. Select Quantity column
Right-click → Change Type → Whole Number
15. Select GST Rate (%) column
Right-click → Change Type → Whole Number

parts.title: PART D — Add a Month Column
steps: 16. Add Column tab → Custom Column
Name: Month
Formula: = Date.ToText([Invoice Date], "MMM-yyyy")
Click OK
17. Drag the Month column (click header, hold, drag) to position it
next to Invoice Date

parts.title: PART E — Load
steps: 18. Home tab → Close & Load → Close & Load To
Select Table → New worksheet → click OK
EXTENSION EXERCISE
Copy any of the CSV files → rename it Sales_Jul2025.csv → change the
dates inside to July. Now in Excel: Data tab → Refresh All.
The July data appears automatically — this is "build once, refresh forever."
✔  Combine & Transform works when all files have identical structure
✔  Source.Name tells you which file each row came from
✔  For files with different sheet names, use UC-10 approach instead

key_learning: Combine & Transform works when all files have identical structure
Source.Name tells you which file each row came from
For files with different sheet names, use UC-10 approach instead

usecases.title: GST Data Cleanup
usecases.description: Clean a raw GSTR-2B dump, flag ITC unavailable rows, and build supplier-wise and period-wise ITC summaries using three approaches: Reference Queries, Pivot Tables, and optionally DAX measures in the Data Model
usecases.objective: Clean a raw GSTR-2B dump, flag ITC unavailable rows, and build supplier-wise and period-wise ITC summaries using three approaches: Reference Queries, Pivot Tables, and optionally DAX measures in the Data Model.

parts.title: PART A — Load the File
steps: 1. In Excel: DATA tab → Get Data → From File → From Text/CSV
2. Browse to GSTR2B_Mock_Jun2025.csv → click Import
3. Click Transform Data
4. Rename the query to: GSTR2B

parts.title: PART B — Fix Data Types
steps: 5. Hold Ctrl and select these columns:
Invoice Value (INR), Taxable Value (INR), IGST, CGST, SGST, Cess
Right-click any selected column → Change Type → Decimal Number
6. Select Invoice Date column
Home tab → Data Type → Date

parts.title: PART C — Add a Total Tax Column
steps: 7. Add Column tab → Custom Column
Name: Total Tax
Formula: = [IGST] + [CGST] + [SGST]
Click OK
8. Right-click the Total Tax column → Change Type → Decimal Number

parts.title: PART D — Flag ITC Availability
steps: 9. Add Column tab → Conditional Column
Name: ITC Flag
Condition setup:
If   Column Name: ITC Availability  |  Operator: equals  |  Value: Yes
Then: Eligible
Else: Blocked / Pending
Click OK
10. Add Column tab → Conditional Column
Name: Action Required
If   Column Name: ITC Availability  |  Operator: equals  |  Value: Yes
Then: No Action
Else: Follow up with supplier / check filing status
Click OK
APPROACH 1 — REFERENCE QUERIES (Power Query summaries)
Best for: fixed formatted reports, feeding data into other queries or systems

parts.title: PART E — ITC Summary by Status
steps: 11. Right-click the GSTR2B query in left panel → Reference
ℹ  Reference creates a LIVE link to the base query — any cleanup
you do on the base automatically applies here too.
(Duplicate would create a static copy with no link to the source)
Rename to: ITC_Summary_by_Status
12. With ITC_Summary_by_Status active:
Home tab → Group By
In Group By dialog:
Group by: ITC Availability
Add Aggregation:
New column name: Total Taxable Value  |  Operation: Sum  |  Column: Taxable Value (INR)
Click Add Aggregation:
New column name: Total Tax Credit  |  Operation: Sum  |  Column: Total Tax
Click Add Aggregation:
New column name: Invoice Count  |  Operation: Count Rows
Click OK

parts.title: PART F — ITC Summary by Supplier
steps: 13. Right-click the GSTR2B query → Reference → Rename to: ITC_Summary_by_Supplier
14. Home tab → Group By
Group by: Trade Name
Add Aggregation: Total Taxable  |  Sum  |  Taxable Value (INR)
Add Aggregation: Total ITC      |  Sum  |  Total Tax
Add Aggregation: Invoice Count  |  Count Rows
Click OK
15. Click Total ITC column → Home tab → Sort Descending

parts.title: PART G — Load All Queries
steps: 16. Home tab → Close & Load → Close & Load To
Load each query to a separate Table on a New worksheet
APPROACH 2 — PIVOT TABLE (recommended for ad-hoc analysis)
Best for: slicing and dicing interactively — no M code needed after load
Prerequisite: complete Parts A–D above first, then load as below
17. Instead of loading to a Table, load GSTR2B to the Data Model:
Home tab → Close & Load → Close & Load To
Select: Only Create Connection
Check the box: Add this data to the Data Model
Click OK
18. In Excel: Insert tab → PivotTable
Select: Use this workbook's Data Model
Choose: New Worksheet → click OK
19. In the PivotTable Fields panel, drag:
Rows:    ITC Availability → Trade Name
Columns: GSTR-1 Filing Period
Values:  Taxable Value (INR) — set to Sum
Total Tax — set to Sum (add a second value)
Filters: Invoice Type (drag here to filter Regular / Credit Note)
20. Right-click anywhere in the Pivot → Add Slicer
Add slicers for: ITC Availability, Place of Supply, GSTR-1 Filing Period
→ One click on a slicer instantly filters the entire summary
21. To add a custom formula inside the Pivot (Calculated Field):
PivotTable Analyze tab → Fields, Items & Sets → Calculated Field
Name: ITC %
Formula: = 'Total Tax' / 'Taxable Value (INR)'
Click OK
→ ITC as a % of taxable value now appears as a column in the Pivot
APPROACH 3 — DAX MEASURES (advanced — Power Pivot / Power BI)
DAX (Data Analysis Expressions) is the formula language of the Data Model.
It looks like Excel formulas but works on entire columns, not individual cells.
This is a brief introduction — DAX is a full topic on its own.
Prerequisite: GSTR2B must be loaded to the Data Model (step 17 above)
22. Power Pivot tab → Manage (opens the Power Pivot window)
If Power Pivot tab is not visible:
File → Options → Add-ins → COM Add-ins → check Microsoft Power Pivot → OK
23. In the Power Pivot window, click the GSTR2B table tab at the bottom
Click in an empty cell in the calculation area (below the data rows)
24. Type this measure and press Enter:
ITC Eligible Amount :=
CALCULATE(
SUM(GSTR2B[Taxable Value (INR)]),
GSTR2B[ITC Availability] = "Yes"
25. Add another measure:
ITC Blocked Amount :=
CALCULATE(
SUM(GSTR2B[Taxable Value (INR)]),
GSTR2B[ITC Availability] = "No"
26. Add a ratio measure:
ITC Eligible % :=
DIVIDE([ITC Eligible Amount], SUM(GSTR2B[Taxable Value (INR)]))
27. Return to Excel → these measures now appear in the PivotTable Fields panel
Drag them into the Values area — they update dynamically with every slicer click
ℹ  DAX vs Pivot Calculated Field:
Calculated Field (step 21) = simple row-level formula, limited capability
DAX Measure = dynamic, filter-aware, handles complex business logic
For CA work: use DAX when you need ratios, YoY comparisons, or
conditional aggregations that Calculated Fields cannot handle.
WHICH APPROACH TO USE?
│ Situation           │ Use                                              │
│ Quick ITC summary   │ Approach 2 — Pivot Table with slicers            │
│ Fixed formatted MIS │ Approach 1 — Reference Queries → Table output    │
│ % ratios in Pivot   │ Approach 2 — Pivot Calculated Field              │
│ Complex KPIs / YoY  │ Approach 3 — DAX Measures in Data Model         │
│ Feed another query  │ Approach 1 — Reference Query output              │
✔  Power Query's job: clean and shape — not calculate business KPIs
✔  Pivot Table + slicers = fastest ad-hoc ITC analysis, no extra queries needed
✔  Reference Query = live link to base; Duplicate = static copy
✔  DAX is the next layer after Power Query — it lives in the Data Model
✔  Keep the layers separate: PQ cleans → Data Model calculates → Pivot presents

key_learning: Power Query's job: clean and shape — not calculate business KPIs
Pivot Table + slicers = fastest ad-hoc ITC analysis, no extra queries needed
Reference Query = live link to base; Duplicate = static copy
DAX is the next layer after Power Query — it lives in the Data Model
Keep the layers separate: PQ cleans → Data Model calculates → Pivot presents

usecases.title: MIS Automation
usecases.description: Transform a messy wide-format Trial Balance (months as columns) into a clean tall format, then enrich with account classifications from the Chart of Accounts
usecases.objective: Transform a messy wide-format Trial Balance (months as columns) into a clean tall format, then enrich with account classifications from the Chart of Accounts.

parts.title: PART A — Load the Trial Balance
steps: 1. In Excel: DATA tab → Get Data → From File → From Excel Workbook
2. Browse to Trial_Balance_Messy.xlsx → click Import
3. A Navigator pane opens showing sheet names
4. Select Sheet1 (or the TB sheet) → click Transform Data
5. Power Query Editor opens — you will see the messy structure with
company name and blank rows at the top, and merged-looking headers
6. Rename the query to: TrialBalance

parts.title: PART B — Remove Junk Rows at Top
steps: 7. In Power Query Editor:
Home tab → Remove Rows → Remove Top Rows
In the dialog: enter 4 → click OK
This removes in one step: company name, report title, blank row,
and the "(Amount in INR)" note row.

parts.title: PART C — Promote Headers
steps: 9. Home tab → Use First Row as Headers
Column names now show:
Account Code | Account Name | April 2025 | Column4 | May 2025 | Column6 | June 2025 | Column8
ℹ  Why Column4, Column6, Column8? The original Excel has merged cells for
each month (spanning Debit + Credit columns). Power Query unmerges them —
the first column gets the month name, the second gets a placeholder name.
10. The first data row is now the Debit/Credit sub-header row (null, null, Debit, Credit…)
Home tab → Remove Rows → Remove Top Rows → enter 1 → click OK
11. Rename columns to combine month + Dr/Cr into one clear name:
Double-click "April 2025" column header → type Apr-25 Debit → Enter
Double-click "Column4"    column header → type Apr-25 Credit → Enter
Double-click "May 2025"   column header → type May-25 Debit → Enter
Double-click "Column6"    column header → type May-25 Credit → Enter
Double-click "June 2025"  column header → type Jun-25 Debit → Enter
Double-click "Column8"    column header → type Jun-25 Credit → Enter
ℹ  These names matter — after Unpivot (Part E), the Attribute column will
contain exactly these names and you will split them on the space to get
Period (Apr-25) and Dr_Cr (Debit/Credit) as separate columns.

parts.title: PART D — Remove Blank Separator Rows
steps: 12. Click the dropdown arrow on the Account Code column header
Uncheck (null) → click OK
The data file has blank rows separating account groups (Assets, Liabilities,
Income, Expenses). These rows have null in Account Code — filtering them out
leaves only the actual account rows.

parts.title: PART E — Unpivot the Month Columns
steps: 13. Click the Account Code column header to select it
Hold Ctrl → click Account Name column header
Both columns are now selected (highlighted)
14. Right-click either selected column header
Click Unpivot Other Columns
15. Two new columns appear:
Attribute — contains "Apr-25 Debit", "Apr-25 Credit", "May-25 Debit" etc.
Value — contains the amounts
16. Rename the Attribute column:
Double-click Attribute column header → type Period_DrCr → Enter
17. Rename the Value column:
Double-click Value → type Amount → Enter

parts.title: PART F — Split Period and Dr/Cr
steps: 18. Select the Period_DrCr column
Home tab → Split Column → By Delimiter
In the dialog:
Select or enter delimiter: Space (select "Space" from dropdown)
Split at: Right-most delimiter
Click OK
You now get: Period_DrCr.1 (e.g. "Apr-25") and Period_DrCr.2 (e.g. "Debit")
19. Rename Period_DrCr.1 to: Period
20. Rename Period_DrCr.2 to: Dr_Cr

parts.title: PART G — Fix Data Type
steps: 21. Select the Amount column
Home tab → Data Type → Decimal Number

parts.title: PART H — Load Chart of Accounts
steps: 22. Home tab → New Source → Text/CSV
23. Browse to Chart_of_Accounts.csv → click Import → click OK
24. Rename this query to: ChartOfAccounts

parts.title: PART I — Merge to Enrich Trial Balance
steps: 25. In the left panel, click TrialBalance to make it active
26. Home tab → Merge Queries → Merge Queries
27. In Merge dialog:
Top table: TrialBalance — click Account Code column
Bottom dropdown: select ChartOfAccounts
Click Account Code column in ChartOfAccounts
Join Kind: Left Outer (all rows from left, matching from right)
Click OK
28. A new ChartOfAccounts column appears at the right
Click the expand icon (two arrows) on the ChartOfAccounts column header
Uncheck "Use original column name as prefix"
Check only: Account Group, Account Type, Nature
Click OK

parts.title: PART J — Load
steps: 29. Home tab → Close & Load → Close & Load To
Table → New worksheet → OK
30. In Excel, insert a Pivot Table from this data:
Rows: Account Group → Account Name
Columns: Period
Values: Sum of Amount
→ Your MIS report updates with one click every month
✔  Unpivot Other Columns is the single most powerful step for accounting data
✔  Always select the columns to KEEP, then unpivot everything else
✔  Merge (Left Outer) enriches every row without VLOOKUP or INDEX-MATCH

key_learning: Unpivot Other Columns is the single most powerful step for accounting data
Always select the columns to KEEP, then unpivot everything else
Merge (Left Outer) enriches every row without VLOOKUP or INDEX-MATCH

usecases.title: Aged Payables
usecases.description: Build an automated aged payables report — vendor-wise outstanding balances, aging bucket summary, and overdue flagging — with one-click refresh
usecases.objective: Build an automated aged payables report — vendor-wise outstanding balances, aging bucket summary, and overdue flagging — with one-click refresh.

parts.title: PART A — Load the File
steps: 1. In Excel: DATA tab → Get Data → From File → From Text/CSV
2. Browse to Aged_Payables_Jun2025.csv → Import → Transform Data
3. Rename the query to: AgedPayables

parts.title: PART B — Fix Data Types
steps: 4. Select Invoice Date column → Home tab → Data Type → Date
5. Select Due Date column → Home tab → Data Type → Date
6. Hold Ctrl and select:
Invoice Amount (INR), Amount Paid (INR), Balance Outstanding (INR)
Right-click → Change Type → Decimal Number
7. Select Overdue Days column → Right-click → Change Type → Whole Number

parts.title: PART C — Flag Overdue Severity
steps: 8. Add Column tab → Conditional Column
Name: Overdue Flag
Condition 1: If  Overdue Days  is greater than  90  Then  Critical
Click Add Clause
Condition 2: If  Overdue Days  is greater than  30  Then  Overdue
Else: Current
Click OK

parts.title: PART D — Vendor-wise Outstanding Summary
steps: 9. Right-click AgedPayables in the left panel → Reference
ℹ  Reference creates a LIVE link to the base query — any cleanup
you do on the base automatically applies here too.
(Duplicate would create a static copy with no link to the source)
Rename to: Vendor_Summary
10. With Vendor_Summary active:
Home tab → Group By
In Group By dialog — click Advanced
Group by: Vendor Name
Click Add Grouping → Vendor GSTIN
Aggregations:
Total Invoiced     |  Sum  |  Invoice Amount (INR)
Total Paid         |  Sum  |  Amount Paid (INR)
Total Outstanding  |  Sum  |  Balance Outstanding (INR)
Invoice Count      |  Count Rows
Click OK
11. Click Total Outstanding column → Home tab → Sort Descending
Highest outstanding vendors appear at top

parts.title: PART E — Aging Bucket Summary
steps: 12. Right-click AgedPayables → Duplicate
Rename to: Aging_Summary
13. Home tab → Group By
Group by: Aging Bucket
Aggregations:
Total Outstanding  |  Sum  |  Balance Outstanding (INR)
Invoice Count      |  Count Rows
Click OK

parts.title: PART F — Critical Overdue List
steps: 14. Right-click AgedPayables → Duplicate
Rename to: Critical_Followup
15. Click dropdown on Overdue Flag column
Uncheck everything except Critical → OK
This is your immediate collections follow-up list with contact details

parts.title: PART G — Load All
steps: 16. Home tab → Close & Load → Close & Load To
Load each query to a separate Table on a New worksheet
✔  Reference Query creates a live link — all summaries update when AgedPayables refreshes
✔  Group By with Advanced mode allows grouping by multiple columns
✔  Sort Descending on Outstanding immediately shows priority vendors

key_learning: Reference Query creates a live link — all summaries update when AgedPayables refreshes
Group By with Advanced mode allows grouping by multiple columns
Sort Descending on Outstanding immediately shows priority vendors

usecases.title: TDS Reconciliation
usecases.description: Reconcile TDS as per books against 26AS — flag mismatches, identify entries not in 26AS, and build a section-wise TDS summary
usecases.objective: Reconcile TDS as per books against 26AS — flag mismatches, identify entries not in 26AS, and build a section-wise TDS summary.

parts.title: PART A — Load the File
steps: 1. In Excel: DATA tab → Get Data → From File → From Text/CSV
2. Browse to TDS_Reconciliation_Q1FY26.csv → Import → Transform Data
3. Rename the query to: TDS_Recon

parts.title: PART B — Fix Data Types
steps: 4. Hold Ctrl and select:
Amount Paid/Credited (INR), TDS Deducted (INR), TDS as per Books (INR),
Difference (INR)
Right-click → Change Type → Decimal Number
5. Select TDS Rate (%) column → Right-click → Change Type → Decimal Number
6. Select Date of Credit/Payment column → Home tab → Data Type → Date

parts.title: PART C — Add Absolute Difference Column
steps: 7. Add Column tab → Custom Column
Name: Difference (Abs)
Formula: = Number.Abs([#"Difference (INR)"])
Click OK
Right-click the new column → Change Type → Decimal Number

parts.title: PART D — Add Action Column
steps: 8. Add Column tab → Conditional Column
Name: Action Required
Condition 1: If  26AS Status  equals  Matched  Then  No Action
Click Add Clause
Condition 2: If  26AS Status  equals  Not in 26AS  Then  Follow up with deductor
Click Add Clause
Condition 3: If  26AS Status  equals  Amount Mismatch  Then  Verify TDS rate
Else: Pending — recheck next month
Click OK

parts.title: PART E — Exception Report (non-matched rows only)
steps: 9. Right-click TDS_Recon in the left panel → Reference
ℹ  Reference creates a LIVE link to the base query — any cleanup
you do on the base automatically applies here too.
(Duplicate would create a static copy with no link to the source)
Rename to: TDS_Exceptions
10. Click dropdown on 26AS Status column
Uncheck Matched → click OK
Only mismatches, pending, and not-in-26AS rows remain
11. Click Difference (Abs) column header
Home tab → Sort Descending
Highest value mismatches appear first — these are priority items

parts.title: PART F — Section-wise TDS Summary
steps: 12. Right-click TDS_Recon → Reference → Rename to: Section_Summary
13. Home tab → Group By → click Advanced
Group by: Section
Click Add Grouping → Nature of Payment
Aggregations:
Amount Paid Total   |  Sum  |  Amount Paid/Credited (INR)
TDS Deducted Total  |  Sum  |  TDS Deducted (INR)
TDS per Books Total |  Sum  |  TDS as per Books (INR)
Total Difference    |  Sum  |  Difference (INR)
Click OK

parts.title: PART G — Load All
steps: 14. Home tab → Close & Load → Close & Load To
Load TDS_Recon, TDS_Exceptions, Section_Summary each to separate worksheets
✔  Number.Abs() in Custom Column — useful for sorting by magnitude of difference
✔  Conditional Column with multiple clauses handles complex business rules cleanly
✔  Exception Report pattern (filter + sort by amount) is your audit priority list

key_learning: Number.Abs() in Custom Column — useful for sorting by magnitude of difference
Conditional Column with multiple clauses handles complex business rules cleanly
Exception Report pattern (filter + sort by amount) is your audit priority list

usecases.title: Expense Claims
usecases.description: Analyse 120 employee expense claims — department spend, rejection patterns, policy breaches, and pending approval list — with full drill-down capability
usecases.objective: Analyse 120 employee expense claims — department spend, rejection patterns, policy breaches, and pending approval list — with full drill-down capability.

parts.title: PART A — Load the File
steps: 1. In Excel: DATA tab → Get Data → From File → From Text/CSV
2. Browse to Expense_Claims_Q1FY26.csv → Import → Transform Data
3. Rename the query to: ExpenseClaims

parts.title: PART B — Fix Data Types
steps: 4. Select Claim Date column → Home tab → Data Type → Date
5. Select Expense Date column → Home tab → Data Type → Date
6. Select Amount Claimed (INR) column → Right-click → Change Type → Decimal Number
7. Select Bill Amount (INR) column → Right-click → Change Type → Decimal Number
8. Note: Approved Amount (INR) column has blanks for Rejected/Pending rows.
Leave it as Text for now — we will handle it in the next step.

parts.title: PART C — Handle Nulls in Approved Amount
steps: 9. Select Approved Amount (INR) column
Transform tab → Replace Values
Value to Find: leave blank (empty)
Replace With: 0
Click OK
10. Now right-click Approved Amount (INR) → Change Type → Decimal Number

parts.title: PART D — Add Variance Column
steps: 11. Add Column tab → Custom Column
Name: Variance
Formula: = [#"Amount Claimed (INR)"] - [#"Approved Amount (INR)"]
Click OK
Right-click Variance → Change Type → Decimal Number

parts.title: PART E — Flag Policy Breaches
steps: 12. Add Column tab → Conditional Column
Name: Approval Outcome
Condition 1: If  Status  equals  Rejected  Then  Rejected
Click Add Clause
Condition 2: If  Variance  is greater than  0  Then  Partial Approval
Else: Full Approval
Click OK

parts.title: PART F — Department Spend Summary
steps: 13. Right-click ExpenseClaims → Reference → Rename to: Dept_Summary
14. Home tab → Group By → click Advanced
Group by: Department
Click Add Grouping → Status
Aggregations:
Total Claimed    |  Sum  |  Amount Claimed (INR)
Total Approved   |  Sum  |  Approved Amount (INR)
Claim Count      |  Count Rows
Click OK

parts.title: PART G — Rejection Analysis
steps: 15. Right-click ExpenseClaims → Reference → Rename to: Rejection_Analysis
16. Click dropdown on Status column → check only Rejected → OK
17. Home tab → Group By
Group by: Rejection Reason
Aggregations:
Count  |  Count Rows
Amount |  Sum  |  Amount Claimed (INR)
Click OK
→ See which rejection reason is most common

parts.title: PART H — Pending Approvals List
steps: 18. Right-click ExpenseClaims → Reference → Rename to: Pending_Approvals
19. Click dropdown on Status → check only Pending → OK
20. Keep columns: Claim ID, Employee Name, Department, Claim Date,
Expense Category, Amount Claimed (INR)
Select these → right-click → Remove Other Columns

parts.title: PART I — Load All
steps: 21. Home tab → Close & Load → Close & Load To
Load each query to a separate Table on a New worksheet
✔  Replace Values handles blanks before type conversion — always do this first
✔  Reference Query + Filter = instant drill-down reports — all linked to one source load
✔  Rejection Analysis pattern reveals process improvement opportunities

key_learning: Replace Values handles blanks before type conversion — always do this first
Reference Query + Filter = instant drill-down reports — all linked to one source load
Rejection Analysis pattern reveals process improvement opportunities

usecases.title: Salary Register
usecases.description: Process the monthly salary register — department-wise payroll cost, statutory compliance check (PF/ESI eligibility), and CTC analysis
usecases.objective: Process the monthly salary register — department-wise payroll cost, statutory compliance check (PF/ESI eligibility), and CTC analysis.

parts.title: PART A — Load the File
steps: 1. In Excel: DATA tab → Get Data → From File → From Text/CSV
2. Browse to Salary_Register_Jun2025.csv → Import → Transform Data
3. Rename the query to: SalaryRegister

parts.title: PART B — Fix Data Types (Bulk)
steps: 4. Hold Ctrl and click each of these column headers:
Basic Salary (INR), HRA (INR), Conveyance Allowance (INR),
Special Allowance (INR), Gross Salary (INR),
PF Employee (12%) (INR), ESI Employee (0.75%) (INR),
Professional Tax (INR), TDS (INR), Total Deductions (INR),
Net Salary Payable (INR), PF Employer (12%) (INR),
ESI Employer (3.25%) (INR), Total CTC (INR)
5. Right-click any selected column → Change Type → Decimal Number
6. Select Date of Joining → Home tab → Data Type → Date

parts.title: PART C — ESI Compliance Check
steps: 7. Add Column tab → Conditional Column
Name: ESI Eligible
Condition: If  Gross Salary (INR)  is less than or equal to  21000
Then: Yes
Else: No
Click OK
8. Add Column tab → Conditional Column
Name: ESI Compliance Check
Condition 1: If  ESI Eligible  equals  Yes  AND ...
(Simplified approach — use Custom Column instead):
Add Column tab → Custom Column
Name: ESI Compliance Check
Formula: = if [ESI Eligible] = "Yes" and [#"ESI Employee (0.75%) (INR)"] = 0
then "ERROR — ESI not deducted"
else if [ESI Eligible] = "No" and [#"ESI Employee (0.75%) (INR)"] > 0
then "ERROR — ESI deducted incorrectly"
else "OK"
Click OK

parts.title: PART D — Employer Cost Column
steps: 9. Add Column tab → Custom Column
Name: Total Employer Cost
Formula: = [#"PF Employer (12%) (INR)"] + [#"ESI Employer (3.25%) (INR)"]
Click OK
Right-click → Change Type → Decimal Number

parts.title: PART E — Department Payroll Summary
steps: 10. Right-click SalaryRegister → Reference → Rename to: Dept_Payroll_Summary
11. Home tab → Group By → click Advanced
Group by: Department
Aggregations:
Employee Count      |  Count Rows
Total Gross         |  Sum  |  Gross Salary (INR)
Total Net Payable   |  Sum  |  Net Salary Payable (INR)
Total PF (Emp)      |  Sum  |  PF Employee (12%) (INR)
Total PF (Er)       |  Sum  |  PF Employer (12%) (INR)
Total CTC           |  Sum  |  Total CTC (INR)
Click OK

parts.title: PART F — Load All
steps: 12. Home tab → Close & Load → Close & Load To
Load SalaryRegister and Dept_Payroll_Summary each to a new worksheet
13. On the SalaryRegister sheet in Excel, add a filter on
ESI Compliance Check = ERROR to instantly find compliance issues
✔  Bulk type change — select all INR columns together, change once
✔  Nested if in Custom Column handles multi-condition compliance checks
✔  Employer Cost as a column shows true payroll burden beyond gross salary

key_learning: Bulk type change — select all INR columns together, change once
Nested if in Custom Column handles multi-condition compliance checks
Employer Cost as a column shows true payroll burden beyond gross salary

usecases.title: Inventory Movement
usecases.description: Combine two months of stock data, calculate movement trends, flag items below reorder level, and build category-wise stock valuation summaries
usecases.objective: Combine two months of stock data, calculate movement trends, flag items below reorder level, and build category-wise stock valuation summaries.

parts.title: PART A — Load April File
steps: 1. In Excel: DATA tab → Get Data → From File → From Text/CSV
2. Browse to Stock_Register_Apr2025.csv → Import → Transform Data
3. Rename the query to: Stock_Apr

parts.title: PART B — Add Month Column to April Query
steps: 4. Add Column tab → Custom Column
Name: Month
Formula: = "Apr-2025"
Click OK

parts.title: PART C — Fix Data Types for April
steps: 5. Hold Ctrl and select:
Opening Qty, Receipts Qty, Issues Qty, Closing Qty, Reorder Level
Right-click → Change Type → Whole Number
6. Hold Ctrl and select:
Unit Cost (INR), Closing Stock Value (INR)
Right-click → Change Type → Decimal Number
7. Select Stock Entry Date → Home tab → Data Type → Date

parts.title: PART D — Load May File
steps: 8. Home tab → New Source → Text/CSV
9. Browse to Stock_Register_May2025.csv → Import → click OK
10. Rename to: Stock_May
11. Repeat steps 4–7 above for Stock_May, changing the Month formula to:

parts.title: PART E — Append Both Months
steps: 12. In the left panel, click Stock_Apr to make it active
13. Home tab → Append Queries → Append Queries as New
14. In Append dialog:
Select: Two tables
First table: Stock_Apr
Table to append: Stock_May
Click OK
15. A new query appears — rename it to: Stock_Combined
16. You now have 200 rows covering both months

parts.title: PART F — Reorder Alert Flag
steps: 17. With Stock_Combined active:
Add Column tab → Conditional Column
Name: Reorder Alert
Condition: If  Closing Qty  is less than or equal to  Reorder Level
Then: REORDER NOW
Else: OK
Click OK

parts.title: PART G — Category Stock Valuation Summary
steps: 18. Right-click Stock_Combined → Reference → Rename to: Category_Summary
19. Home tab → Group By → click Advanced
Group by: Category
Click Add Grouping → Month
Aggregations:
Total Stock Value  |  Sum  |  Closing Stock Value (INR)
Total Receipts     |  Sum  |  Receipts Qty
Total Issues       |  Sum  |  Issues Qty
Click OK
→ Month-over-month stock value by category

parts.title: PART H — Reorder List
steps: 20. Right-click Stock_Combined → Reference → Rename to: Reorder_List
21. Click dropdown on Reorder Alert → check only REORDER NOW → OK
22. Select columns to keep: Item Code, Item Description, Category,
Closing Qty, Reorder Level, Unit Cost (INR), Supplier Code, Location
Right-click → Remove Other Columns
23. Click Closing Qty column → Home tab → Sort Ascending
Items furthest below reorder level appear first

parts.title: PART I — Load All
steps: 24. Home tab → Close & Load → Close & Load To
Load Stock_Combined, Category_Summary, Reorder_List each to new worksheets
(You can choose not to load Stock_Apr and Stock_May to sheets —
right-click them → Load To → Only Create Connection)
✔  Add Month column BEFORE appending — so you know which month each row is from
✔  Append (not Merge) = stacking rows from tables with same structure
✔  Only Create Connection for helper queries — keeps your workbook clean

key_learning: Add Month column BEFORE appending — so you know which month each row is from
Append (not Merge) = stacking rows from tables with same structure
Only Create Connection for helper queries — keeps your workbook clean

usecases.title: Multi Year Order Analysis
usecases.description: Combine 4 years of order data from Excel files with INCONSISTENT sheet names, fix dirty data in a numeric column, split a compound field, and build a year-over-year performance summary
usecases.objective: Combine 4 years of order data from Excel files with INCONSISTENT sheet names, fix dirty data in a numeric column, split a compound field, and build a year-over-year performance summary.

parts.title: PART A — Load the Folder
steps: 1. In Excel: DATA tab → Get Data → From File → From Folder
2. Browse to this folder (UC-10-Multi-Year-Order-Analysis) → click OK
3. A preview window shows all 4 Excel files listed
4. Click Transform Data (NOT Combine — very important)
5. Power Query Editor opens with 4 rows — one per file
6. Rename the query to: OrderData

parts.title: PART B — Remove Unnecessary Columns
steps: 7. Right-click the Content column header → select Remove Other Columns
Wait — we need Name too. Instead:
Hold Ctrl → click Content column AND Name column to select both
Right-click → Remove Other Columns
You now have only 2 columns: Content and Name

parts.title: PART C — Open Each File as an Excel Workbook
steps: 8. Add Column tab → Custom Column
Name: Workbook
Formula: = Excel.Workbook([Content], true)
Click OK
(Note: you must type this formula — the Available Columns panel
only inserts column references, not function calls)

parts.title: PART D — Expand the Workbook Column
steps: 9. Click the expand icon (two arrows) on the Workbook column header
In the dropdown, leave ALL columns checked → click OK
You now see columns: Workbook.Name, Workbook.Data, Workbook.Item,
Workbook.Kind, Workbook.Hidden
10. You may see more than 4 rows — some files have named ranges too

parts.title: PART E — Filter to Sheets Only  *** KEY STEP ***
steps: 11. Click the dropdown arrow on the Workbook.Kind column header
Uncheck everything EXCEPT Sheet → click OK
You now have exactly 4 rows — one per file
This step works regardless of what the sheet is named

parts.title: PART F — Remove Helper Columns
steps: 12. Hold Ctrl and click to select: Content, Workbook.Name,
Workbook.Item, Workbook.Kind, Workbook.Hidden
Right-click → Remove Columns
You should now have only: Name and Workbook.Data

parts.title: PART G — Extract Year from Filename
steps: 13. Select the Name column
Add Column tab → Extract → First Characters
Count: 4 → click OK
A new column appears with: 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020
14. Rename the new column: double-click its header → type Year → Enter

parts.title: PART H — Expand the Data
steps: 15. Click the expand icon on the Workbook.Data column header
Uncheck "Use original column name as prefix"
Leave all columns checked → click OK
All ~3,200 rows from all 4 years now appear in one table

parts.title: PART I — Fix Data Types
steps: 16. Home tab → Data Type on each column:
Order Date → Date
Ship Date  → Date  (2020 rows will show null — that is correct, Open orders)
Revenue    → Decimal Number
Cost       → Decimal Number
Cookies Shipped → Whole Number
17. When you change Revenue to Decimal Number, some rows in 2017
will show an error (the "mn" value in the data)
Click the word Error in any error cell to see what happened

parts.title: PART J — Fix the Dirty Revenue Data
steps: 18. Select the Revenue column
Transform tab → Replace Errors
Value: 0
Click OK
The errors are now replaced with 0

parts.title: PART K — Split Customer ID
steps: 19. Select the Customer ID column
Home tab → Split Column → By Delimiter
In the dialog:
Delimiter: Custom → type  -  (space hyphen space)
Split at: Left-most delimiter
Click OK
Two columns appear: Customer ID.1 and Customer ID.2
20. Rename Customer ID.1 → Customer Code
Rename Customer ID.2 → Customer Name

parts.title: PART L — Add Derived Columns
steps: 21. Add Column tab → Custom Column
Name: Profit
Formula: = [Revenue] - [Cost]
Click OK → Change Type → Decimal Number
22. Add Column tab → Custom Column
Name: Margin %
Formula: = if [Revenue] = 0 then 0 else [Profit] / [Revenue]
Click OK → Change Type → Decimal Number
23. Add Column tab → Custom Column
Name: Fulfilment Days
Formula: = Duration.Days([Ship Date] - [Order Date])
Click OK → Change Type → Whole Number
(Rows where Ship Date is null will show null — correct for Open orders)

parts.title: PART M — Year-over-Year Summary
steps: 24. Right-click OrderData → Reference → Rename to: YoY_Summary
25. Home tab → Group By
Group by: Year
Aggregations:
Total Orders    |  Count Rows
Total Revenue   |  Sum  |  Revenue
Total Cost      |  Sum  |  Cost
Total Profit    |  Sum  |  Profit
Click OK
26. Click Year column → Home tab → Sort Ascending

parts.title: PART N — Load All
steps: 27. Home tab → Close & Load → Close & Load To
Load OrderData and YoY_Summary each to a new worksheet
NOTE — Power BI Users
All steps above work identically in Power BI Desktop (free download from
microsoft.com). No Excel license required. The Get Data → Folder option
and all M functions are the same in both tools.
✔  Filter Kind = Sheet sidesteps sheet name inconsistency entirely
✔  Excel.Workbook() is a function — must be typed, not just inserted
✔  Replace Errors after type change = standard dirty data handling pattern
✔  Duration.Days() handles date arithmetic; nulls flow through gracefully
✔  Never use Combine & Transform when files have different sheet names

key_learning: Filter Kind = Sheet sidesteps sheet name inconsistency entirely
Excel.Workbook() is a function — must be typed, not just inserted
Replace Errors after type change = standard dirty data handling pattern
Duration.Days() handles date arithmetic; nulls flow through gracefully
Never use Combine & Transform when files have different sheet names
